events.international: [concert] 1918-04-17 to 1922-04-23 — New Orleans, LA. Asked by Fletcher Henderson - on tour through New Orleans with Ethel Waters - to join the Waters / Henderson touring group; declined the offer, giving as reason that his friend Zutty Singleton didn't want to leave New Orleans Context: In the late 1910s, New Orleans remains a key origin-point for jazz as musicians begin migrating to larger northern markets.
events.usContext: In 1918, the U.S. military remains segregated; Black soldiers face discrimination even as they serve in World War I.

events.international: [concert] 1922-07-05 — Chicago, IL. Received telegram from King Oliver with an offer to join Oliver's orchestra in Chicago. Louis got the telegram during a funeral parade with the Tuxedo Brass Band. The deceased (death occurred July 2, 1922) was the father of musician Eddie Vincent. Note: According to Wright, Berrett and others LA received the telegram July 22, 1922 and arrived in Chicago, IL August 8, 1922. LA himself has given both dates. Harker p.65 gives very plausible arguments for LA:s move to Chicago as listed here. Context: Prohibition-era Chicago supports a dense club scene; jazz becomes a defining sound of urban nightlife.
events.usContext: In 1922, segregation and discriminatory policing remain central realities of American public life.

events.international: [concert] 1928-07-02 — Chicago, IL — Chicago Theatre and visited Bix Beiderbecke backstage after the show. Chicago, IL. Louis attended the Paul Whiteman opening night at the Chicago Theatre and visited Bix Beiderbecke backstage after the show. Bix and some other Whiteman members met Louis at the location he was performing (probably the Savoy Ballroom) where they jammed together after Louis had finished for the night. [concert] 1949-04-21 to 1949-05-17 — New York, NY — Roxy Theatre. At Roxy Theatre (New York, NY): Roxy Theatre. (DB April 7 & April 21 & May 5 & June 30, 1950; Waters; Estrad June 1950) Notes: 1) DB June 30 says that Louis was sick in ulcers every day at this engagement but that he nevertheless played. 2) Initially this engagement was to last April 21-28, but this soon was changed to end May 4. It seems however that Louis remained until much later, probably to May 17 – see note to May 13, 1950 below. Context: Postwar New York booms as a media capital with radio and television; jazz clubs persist amid new entertainment formats.

[concert] 1949-06-16 to 1949-07-06 — New York, NY — Bop City. At Bop City (New York, NY): Bop City. Feather (article dated July 10): 1) Numerous parties for Louis including a show July 4 at Bop City with lots of celebrities. He also did a TV show. 2) Louis has a serious disease and is presently examined by an expert who will decide if an operation is necessary. Dahlgren: I wasn't there July 4, but a few days earlier, and found Armstrong and the band at their best. Full houses. Note: the date for the start of this engagement has been given as variously June 16, June 23 and June 26. The exact closing date is not certified either. Context: Postwar New York booms as a media capital with radio and television; jazz clubs persist amid new entertainment formats.

events.international: [concert] 1949-10-10 — New York, NY. New York, Dumont-TV, "Cavalcade Of Bands". Probably October 17-? San Francisco, CA, New Orleans Swing Club. (DB July 14 & Nov 3 & Dec 1, 1950) Note: not quite certain that this date was fulfilled. DB for July 14, 1950 reported that the owner of the club had been arrested for being a narcotics operator and had closed down the club but DB also mentioned that Louis had a contract for a New Orleans Swing Club start October 17, 1950. Further, DB November 3, 1950 lists an engagement at the club, starting October 17, 1950 and DB December 1 says that Louis possibly will be back in the club in January 1951, so it seems the club wasn't closed after all. Possibly November 9 * New York, NBC-TV, "Kay Kyser's College Of Musical Knowledge".. Hägglöf gives the date November 9, 1950 on a CD with recordings from a tape from Bob Hilbert. The date may therefore origin from Hilbert. Stratemann puts the generally accepted date November 19, 1950 in doubt and gives an October 13, 1950 date s an alternative, adding that both these dates are unconfirmed. Context: Postwar New York booms as a media capital with radio and television; jazz clubs persist amid new entertainment formats.
events.usContext: In 1949, segregated housing and schooling remain central battlegrounds as civil-rights litigation expands.

[concert] 1950-06-15 to 1950-07-05 — Chicago, IL — Blue Note. At Blue Note (Chicago, IL): Blue Note. (DB May 18 & July 13, 1951; Frank Holzfeind's notes) Holzfeind: $ 4.000 per week, 32 hours per five day week; Dale Jones came in for Arvell Shaw for the 2nd set June 24 and remained for the rest of the engagement; Billie Holiday shared the engagement, at least for some part of the engagement. Context: Postwar Chicago remains a major stop on U.S. touring routes as arenas, ballrooms, and broadcast-driven entertainment expand in the TV era.
events.usContext: In 1950, the NAACP intensifies litigation strategy that soon culminates in Brown v. Board of Education.

events.international: [concert] 1952-08-16 to 1952-08-22 — Atlantic City, NJ — Steel Pier. At Steel Pier (Atlantic City, NJ): Steel Pier. Possibly August 23 Washington, DC, outdoor concert. Hinton describes this event very amusingly. It was a joint concert with the Lionel Hampton orchestra and Illinois Jacquet's band and it occurred before September 4 when Hampton went abroad for a long tour. Most probably it was a date in the period August 23-31, 1953. Possibly the venue was Watergate Amphitheatre, Washington, DC. Context: While in Atlantic City, NJ, Eisenhower is elected U.S. president (1952) amid Cold War anxieties.
events.usContext: In 1952, the Supreme Court hears arguments in the school segregation cases that will become Brown v. Board of Education.

events.international: [concert] 1952-11-26 to 1952-11-30 — Chicago, IL — Blue Note. At Blue Note (Chicago, IL): Blue Note. Armstrong's 5-day-appearance was the last before the club closed down, to reopen at another location four months later. Holzfeind: $ 5.000 per week, 30 hours per five day week, Sunday November 29, matinee. Context: Chicago sits at the crossroads of national touring routes, supporting large venues and media-driven popular music.
events.usContext: In 1952, the Supreme Court hears arguments in the school segregation cases that will become Brown v. Board of Education.

events.international: [concert] 1952-11-30 — Chicago, IL — Blue Note. At Blue Note (Chicago, IL): Blue Note. Blue Note closed for four months, BBC broadcast, last tune played was Auld Lang Syne (Frank Holzfeind's notes) Mid Dec-early Jan 1954 Tour in Japan and on Hawaii. Note: Boujut as well as Jones-Chilton call this a tour to Japan and Australia. No Australian engagements have been found however. Some information indicates that Louis and the All Stars performed on Hawaii before, as well as after, their engagements in Japan. Context: Chicago sits at the crossroads of national touring routes, supporting large venues and media-driven popular music.
events.usContext: In 1952, civil-rights organizations expand pressure for equal schooling, fair employment, and voting rights enforcement.

events.international: [concert] 1952-12-01 — Honolulu, HI. In Honolulu, HI: Louis sits in with Ken Alford's Dixie Cats at Waikiki Tavern. (Info from earlier existing web-site www.bikehawaii.com/jazz/satchmo.html) Either the occasion for Louis' visit at the Waikiki Tavern was when the All Stars were on their way to Japan, in early/mid December 1953, or during their stay in Honolulu in the first weeks of January 1954. Context: While in Honolulu, HI, Television becomes a dominant medium for popular culture.

events.international: [concert] 1952-12-31 — Chicago, IL. Chicago, IL. (DB December 31, 1952). Louis finished on top in a poll among readers to elect the first to enter Down Beat's "Hall of Fame" as the "most important musical personality of all time". Louis got 681 votes while closest runners-up were Duke Ellington (664 votes), Stan Kenton and Glenn Miller. Johann Sebastian Bach finished 7th with 111 votes! Context: Postwar Chicago remains a major stop on U.S. touring routes as arenas, ballrooms, and broadcast-driven entertainment expand in the TV era.

[concert] 1954-01-02 — Honolulu, HI. Honolulu, HI. Louis' wife Lucille was caught in the customs with "a joint in her eye-glass case". Louis and the band were held for hours by the customs and the following day their engagement at a venue called the Brown Derby in Honolulu was cancelled because of the incident. Note: it seems that Louis and the All Stars remained on Hawaii until the beginning of the following engagement in San Francisco since Louis himself was present at Ken Alford's wedding on January 10, 1954. Context: While in Honolulu, HI, Brown v. Board of Education (1954) rules school segregation unconstitutional in the U.S.
events.usContext: In 1954, Brown v. Board of Education rules segregated public schools unconstitutional, a turning point in the civil-rights movement.

events.international: [concert] 1954-04-16 to 1954-04-27 — New York, NY — Basin Street. At Basin Street (New York, NY): Basin Street. NYAN: "Nitely except Monday". Note: Louis may have started the Basin Street engagement one week earlier, April 9, 1955 but other artists were definitely at Basin Street April 29-30. Note: In the booklet notes to CD Mr Music MMCD-9003 "Louis Armstrong All Staras 'Live' at Basin St." Wayne Knight gives the dates April 7 through April 27 for Armstrong's Basin Street engagement. Probably April 16-27 * New York, NY, Basin Street, pre-recording of four NBC broadcast transcriptions, "All Star Parade Of Bands". These shows were broadcast May 7, May 14, May 21 and May 28, 1955 in benefit of the Savings Bonds Division, US Treasury Department with Fred Collins announcer. Notes: 1) one or more of these broadcasts may have been pre-recorded during Armstrong's next engagement at Basin Street that started May 5, 1955. 2) a report of a fifth "All Star Parade Of Bands" show from Basin Street with broadcast date June 4, 1955 is incorrect. Context: In New York, television and mass media dominate popular culture, while civil-rights activism grows alongside vibrant urban arts scenes.
events.usContext: In 1954, civil-rights activists intensify campaigns to translate legal victories into real-world desegregation.

[concert] 1954-10-05 — New Orleans, LA. Publishing date for Louis Armstrong's book "Satchmo. My Life In New Orleans." (DB July 28, 1954) Possibly October 6 * Unknown location, Savings Bonds Division, US Treasury Department, broadcast "Guest Star" # 403. The date October 6, 1954 is engraved in the wax of the Guest Star disc and the label has the instruction "Do Not Play Before December 12, 1954". In Westerberg p.133 is the date January 6, 1954 also mentioned. This, however, is a very unlikely recording date since Louis at that date was appearing on Hawaii. Context: The postwar jazz revival renews interest in New Orleans styles even as modern jazz evolves elsewhere.

Probably early January New York, NY, CBS radio, prerecording of "The Woolworth Hour" with Beverly Sills, soprano of the New York City Opera and Russell Arms, radio and television tenor. (PC Jan 21, 1956). PC: Louis Armstrong played with Percy Faith and his orchestra the "Back-o'-Town Blues" and "Some Day You'll Be Sorry." Note: This program was broadcast Sunday January 15, 1956. Since Louis was in Los Angeles on that date it is here assumed that Louis' part of the program was prerecorded in New York before he left for California. Early Jan-early February Los Angeles, CA, MGM film "High Society". Context: New York’s jazz-and-club circuit continues amid Cold War politics and the rise of modern civil-rights organizing.

[concert] 1956-07-09 — New York — Columbia recording session. In a letter to Joe Glaser George Avakian says that he had made reservations for studio recordings starting July 9, 1956 if conditions, weather or other, at the Newport Jazz Festival made live recordings of sufficiently good quality impossible. Since microphone troubles disturbed Armstrong’s concert a studio recording at one or more of the available days is possible. (it is known that parts of Duke Ellington’s concert were rerecorded at a studio session July 9, 1956.) Compare Willems p. 275-276

events.international: [concert] 1956-07-14 — New York — Lewisohn Stadium, “Jazz Jamboree” with Louis Armstrong

[concert] 1956-07-14 — New York, NY — Lewisohn Stadium. At Lewisohn Stadium (New York, NY): "Jazz Jamboree" with Louis Armstrong and the All Stars, the Dave Brubeck Quartet and the New York Philharmonic under Leonard Bernstein. Note: This was the first ever jazz night at Lewisohn stadium. St Louis Blues, arranged by Alfredo Antonini, was performed by Armstrong, the All Stars and the Philharmonics as the concert's finale. W.C. Handy, composer of St Louis Blues, was guest of honour. Context: In New York, television and mass media dominate popular culture, while civil-rights activism grows alongside vibrant urban arts scenes.

[concert] 1948-10-12 — Antwerp. Antwerp, Belgium, Feestzaal van de Dierentuin. Context: While in Antwerp, Truman orders desegregation of the U.S. armed forces (1948).

events.international: [concert] 1948-10-13 — Brussels — Palais Des Beaux Arts. Brussels, Belgium, Palais Des Beaux Arts. Context: While in Brussels, The Berlin Airlift (1948–49) becomes a defining early Cold War confrontation.
events.usContext: In 1948, President Truman issues Executive Order 9981 to desegregate the U.S. armed forces.

events.international: [context] Armstrong plays Brussels on his European circuit, performing to an audience that includes members of the Belgian royal court.
events.usContext: Eisenhower is elected president. The United States enters a period of postwar prosperity that largely excludes Black Americans.

events.international: [concert] 1954-10-30 — Brussels — Palais Des Beaux Arts. Brussels, Belgium, Palais Des Beaux Arts, 2 concerts. Context: While in Brussels, The Cold War shapes politics and culture across continents in the mid-1950s.
events.usContext: In 1954, backlash to Brown begins to organize into “massive resistance” across the South.

events.usContext: In 1948, President Truman issues Executive Order 9981 to desegregate the U.S. armed forces.

events.usContext: In 1952, the Supreme Court hears arguments in the school segregation cases that will become Brown v. Board of Education.

events.international: [context] Armstrong visits Helsinki, performing for enthusiastic Finnish audiences at the Finlandia concert series. Finnish jazz fans have followed his recordings for decades.
events.usContext: Montgomery Bus Boycott. The civil rights movement gains momentum across the American South.

events.international: [concert] 1962-05-27 — Helsinki — Mässhall A, one concert. A very tired Louis arrived at Hotel Vaakuna shortly before 15.00

[concert] 1962-05-27 — Helsinki — Mässhall A. Helsinki, Finland, Mässhall A, one concert. A very tired Louis arrived at Hotel Vaakuna shortly before 15.00 and left for manager Ira Mangel to handle the scheduled press conference due to his own need to rest. Louis left Helsinki the following morning at 06.00 for Copenhagen, Denmark. (Hans Westerberg; Hufvudstadsbladet May 28, 1962) Context: While in Helsinki, The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) brings the world close to nuclear war.
events.usContext: In 1962, James Meredith integrates the University of Mississippi amid riots and federal intervention, spotlighting the battle over desegregation.

events.usContext: In 1933, Black organizations press for fair access to relief and jobs amid the Depression.

[politics] Armstrong's visit to Kampala is part of the U.S. State Department's cultural diplomacy program, using jazz as a tool of Cold War influence in Africa.
events.usContext: The U.S. uses cultural ambassadors to counter Soviet influence in newly independent nations, despite its own racial contradictions.

events.international: [concert] 1960-10-31 — Entebbe — one performance. (LAHA)

[concert] 1960-10-31 — Entebbe. Entebbe, Uganda, one performance. (LAHA) prob Nov. 1 or 2 Kampala, Uganda, Nakivubo Stadium. (Ad and concert program on eBay) Context: While in Entebbe, A wave of African independence reshapes global politics in the 'Year of Africa' (1960).
events.usContext: In 1960, the Greensboro sit-ins spark a wave of student-led direct action; SNCC is formed to coordinate activism.

[concert] 1960-11-03 — Entebbe — Nigeria – Nairobi. Travel; Entebbe, Nigeria – Nairobi, Kenya and Nairobi, Kenya, probably Nairobi City Hall, two performances. Context: While in Entebbe, A wave of African independence reshapes global politics in the 'Year of Africa' (1960).
events.usContext: In 1960, the Greensboro sit-ins spark a wave of student-led direct action; SNCC is formed to coordinate activism.

events.international: [concert] 1963-04-04 — Hong Kong. Travel Singapore - Hong Kong, China. Accomodation Ambassador Hotel. Note: In 1963 Hong Kong was part of the British Empire. Context: While in Hong Kong, Major global shifts in civil rights and decolonization define the early 1960s.
events.usContext: In 1963, violence and backlash continue—Medgar Evers is assassinated and the 16th Street Baptist Church is bombed—marking an intense year of struggle.

events.international: [concert] 1963-04-05 — Hong Kong — City Hall. (LAHA)
[concert] 1963-04-05 — Hong Kong — City Hall. Hong Kong, China, City Hall. (LAHA) Context: While in Hong Kong, The March on Washington (1963) amplifies U.S. civil-rights demands.
events.usContext: In 1963, the Birmingham campaign and police repression shock the nation, accelerating support for civil-rights legislation.

events.international: [context] Armstrong's recordings reach Chinese audiences through shortwave radio broadcasts, even as official access is restricted during the Cold War.
events.usContext: The March on Washington and Dr. King's "I Have a Dream" speech mark a turning point in the civil rights movement.

events.international: [politics] Armstrong visits Abidjan as part of the West African leg of his State Department goodwill tour, connecting with Ivorian musicians and audiences.
events.usContext: The goodwill tours are designed to promote American democratic values abroad, even as those values are denied to Black Americans at home.

events.international: [concert] 1960-12-02 — Abidjan — Ivory Coast. Travel; Lome, Togo – Abidjan, Ivory Coast. (LAHA) and possibly Abidjan, Ivory Coast, performance(s?). Context: While in Abidjan, The U.S. civil-rights movement grows through sit-ins and mass organizing.
events.usContext: In 1960, sit-ins and boycotts spread across U.S. cities, challenging segregation in everyday spaces.

events.international: [concert] 1960-12-02 — Abidjan — performance(s?)

[concert] 1960-12-03 — Abidjan — Ivory Coast. Abidjan, Ivory Coast, three performances. (LAHA) and Travel; Abidjan, Ivory Coast - Cotonou, Benin, and probably Departure from Cotonou, Benin for France. Note: Today's Benin was in 1960 Dahomey. Note: For the period Early December – Mid January 1961 the two main sources of information are LAHA (to some extent based upon LA:s scrapbooks) and Hugues Panassié in Bulletin Du Hot Club De France No. 106, March 1961. They differ however quite a lot, in which instances we have chosen to rely upon Panassié for the period December 5, 1960 when LA arrived in Paris, France to January 7, 1961 when LA left Paris. Context: While in Abidjan, A wave of African independence reshapes global politics in the 'Year of Africa' (1960).
events.usContext: In 1960, the Greensboro sit-ins spark a wave of student-led direct action; SNCC is formed to coordinate activism.

events.international: [context] Armstrong plays Cairo as the Suez Crisis unfolds, demonstrating American cultural presence during a geopolitically tense moment in the Middle East.
events.usContext: The Suez Crisis creates tension between the U.S. and its European allies. Eisenhower pressures Britain and France to withdraw.

events.international: [concert] 1961-01-27 to 1961-01-28 — Cairo. Cairo, Egypt. (LAHA; LA and Lucille on photo in front of the Sfinx in Gizeh - info from H-G Klauer in letter to HF) Visits hospital orphanage outside Cairo, Egypt and probably Cairo, Egypt, performance(-s) Context: While in Cairo, The Bay of Pigs invasion (1961) escalates U.S.–Cuba tensions.
events.usContext: In 1961, movement strategy increasingly centers on direct action and national media attention.

events.international: [concert] 1961-01-27 — Cairo — performance(-s)
[concert] 1961-01-28 — Cairo — performance(-s)

[concert] 1961-01-29 — Cairo — Egypt – Nice. Probably travel; Cairo, Egypt – Nice, France. Note: LAHA has the following dates that have been disregarded in favour of the Panassié / Stratemann info: December 6, 1960 – Monrovia, Liberia; December 9, 1960 – Freetown, Sierra Leone (Three performances); December 13, 1960 – Conakry, Guinea (Two performances); December 16-17, 1960 – Dakar, Senegal; December 30, 1960 – Guinea; January 3, 1961 – Senegal. Context: While in Cairo, The Berlin Wall begins to rise (1961), symbolizing Cold War division.
events.usContext: In 1961, civil-rights activism escalates as federal officials are pressured to enforce desegregation rulings.

events.international: [context] Armstrong plays Nairobi during the Mau Mau uprising period, connecting with East African audiences hungry for American culture.
events.usContext: The U.S. supports African decolonization in principle while maintaining Cold War alliances with European colonial powers.

events.international: [concert] 1960-11-03 — Nairobi — probably Nairobi City Hall, two performances. (LAHA; Travis p.166)
[concert] 1960-11-04 — Nairobi. Bus from Nairobi, Kenya to Nakuru, Kenya and Nakuru, Kenya, one performance. (LAHA) Context: While in Nairobi, The U-2 incident (1960) heightens U.S.–Soviet tensions.
events.usContext: In 1960, the Civil Rights Act of 1960 is enacted, strengthening federal oversight of voting rights.

events.international: [concert] 1960-11-04 — Nakuru — one performance. (LAHA)
[concert] 1960-11-07 — Nairobi — two performances (LAHA), probably “the African Stadium” (Travis p.166)
[concert] 1960-11-07 — Nairobi. Nairobi, Kenya, two performances (LAHA), probably "the African Stadium". Context: While in Nairobi, The U-2 incident (1960) heightens U.S.–Soviet tensions.
events.usContext: In 1960, the Civil Rights Act of 1960 is enacted, strengthening federal oversight of voting rights.

events.international: [concert] 1960-11-09 — Mombasa — one performance. (LAHA)

[concert] 1960-11-09 — Nairobi — Kenya – Mombasa. Travel; Nairobi, Kenya – Mombasa, Kenya and Mombasa, Kenya, one performance. (LAHA) Context: While in Nairobi, A wave of African independence reshapes global politics in the 'Year of Africa' (1960).
events.usContext: In 1960, the Greensboro sit-ins spark a wave of student-led direct action; SNCC is formed to coordinate activism.

events.international: [context] Armstrong performs in Santiago as part of his Latin American goodwill tour, drawing massive crowds at the Teatro Municipal.
events.usContext: The Civil Rights Act of 1957 passes—the first civil rights bill since Reconstruction—though critics call it insufficient.

events.international: [concert] 1962-06-01 — Santiago — outdoor concert (Stratemann p.478) (acc. to Mr Enrique Planas the All Stars performed in a cirkus tent in downtown Santiago; see also Darensbourg p.174)
[concert] 1962-06-01 — Santiago. Arrival in Santiago, Chile (LAHA) and Santiago, Chile, outdoor concert Context: While in Santiago, Decolonization continues to reshape Africa and Asia.
events.usContext: In 1962, civil-rights campaigns intensify around voting rights and equal access to public institutions.

events.international: [concert] 1962-06-02 — Santiago — outdoor concert (Stratemann p.478; Darensbourg p.174)
[concert] 1962-06-02 — Santiago. Santiago, Chile, outdoor concert Context: While in Santiago, The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) brings the world close to nuclear war.
events.usContext: In 1962, James Meredith integrates the University of Mississippi amid riots and federal intervention, spotlighting the battle over desegregation.

events.international: [context] Armstrong visits Lagos as Nigerian independence movements gain strength. His music connects deeply with Nigerian audiences who see jazz as a Pan-African art form.
events.usContext: Armstrong reportedly considers refusing State Department tours to protest the hypocrisy of promoting democracy abroad while facing racism at home.

events.international: [concert] 1960-10-18 — Lagos — Lagos Stadium, concert (Photo of ticket in Louis Armstrong A Cultural Legacy p.90)
[concert] 1960-10-23 — Kano — probably performance(-s). (Deduction from entries for October 24, 1960 in LA:s scrapbook 58, held at the LAHA)

[concert] 1960-10-23 — Kano. Kano, Nigeria, probably performance(-s). (Deduction from entries for October 24, 1960 in LA:s scrapbook 58, held at the LAHA) Context: While in Kano, The U-2 incident (1960) heightens U.S.–Soviet tensions.
events.usContext: In 1960, the Civil Rights Act of 1960 is enacted, strengthening federal oversight of voting rights.

events.international: [concert] 1960-10-24 — Kano — Nigeria – Lagos. Travel; Kano, Nigeria – Lagos, Nigeria – Doula, Cameroon, and Doula, Cameroon, two performances. Context: While in Kano, The U.S. civil-rights movement grows through sit-ins and mass organizing.
events.usContext: In 1960, sit-ins and boycotts spread across U.S. cities, challenging segregation in everyday spaces.

events.international: Armstrong performs in Dar es Salaam (then Tanganyika), drawing enthusiastic East African crowds to an outdoor concert.
events.usContext: The civil rights movement grows, with school desegregation battles continuing across the American South.

events.international: [concert] 1960-11-11 — Dar-Es-Salaam. Travel; Zanzibar Town, Zanzibar, Tanzania – Dar-Es-Salaam, Tanzania. (LAHA) Context: While in Dar-Es-Salaam, The U.S. civil-rights movement grows through sit-ins and mass organizing.
events.usContext: In 1960, sit-ins and boycotts spread across U.S. cities, challenging segregation in everyday spaces.

events.international: [concert] 1960-11-12 — Dar-Es-Salaam. Dar-Es-Salaam, Tanzania, two performances. (LAHA) Context: While in Dar-Es-Salaam, A wave of African independence reshapes global politics in the 'Year of Africa' (1960).
events.usContext: In 1960, the Greensboro sit-ins spark a wave of student-led direct action; SNCC is formed to coordinate activism.

events.international: Armstrong performs in Lisbon, then under the Salazar dictatorship. The concert is one of the few open cultural exchanges the regime permits.
events.usContext: The U.S. maintains strategic relations with Portugal despite its authoritarian government due to access to the Azores military bases.

events.international: [concert] 1961-02-28 — Lisbon. Lisbon, Portugal. (LAHA) Context: While in Lisbon, The Berlin Wall begins to rise (1961), symbolizing Cold War division.
events.usContext: In 1961, civil-rights activism escalates as federal officials are pressured to enforce desegregation rulings.

events.international: [concert] 1961-03-02 — Lisbon. Lisbon, Portugal. (LAHA) Context: While in Lisbon, Yuri Gagarin becomes the first human in space (1961), intensifying the Space Race.
events.usContext: In 1961, Freedom Riders challenge segregation in interstate bus travel, drawing national attention and violence.

events.international: [concert] 1954-12-28 — Wien. Wien, Austria, Konzerthaus. Context: While in Wien, Brown v. Board of Education (1954) rules school segregation unconstitutional in the U.S.
events.usContext: In 1954, Brown v. Board of Education rules segregated public schools unconstitutional, a turning point in the civil-rights movement.

events.international: Armstrong plays Vienna shortly after Austria regains full sovereignty following Allied occupation. The concert feels like a celebration of freedom.
events.usContext: The Vienna State Treaty restores Austrian independence. Austria declares permanent neutrality in the Cold War.

events.international: [concert] 1956-05-19 — Dublin — National Sports Arena, 2 shows. (Selchow p.355; Stratemann p.616)

[concert] 1956-05-19 — Dublin — National Sports Arena. Dublin, Ireland, National Sports Arena, 2 shows. Context: While in Dublin, The Hungarian Revolution (1956) is crushed, highlighting Soviet control in Eastern Europe.
events.usContext: In 1956, “massive resistance” campaigns spread as courts and local officials clash over Brown’s implementation.

events.international: [context] Armstrong plays Dublin's Gaiety Theatre, connecting with Irish jazz enthusiasts who have followed his recordings for years.
events.usContext: The civil rights movement builds momentum with bus boycotts and Supreme Court victories.

events.international: [context] Armstrong performs in Havana, connecting with Cuba's vibrant Afro-Cuban jazz scene. The cross-pollination influences his music for years.
events.usContext: Cold War tensions begin as the U.S. and Soviet Union square off over the future of Europe.

events.international: [context] Armstrong's final pre-Revolution visit to Cuba. Castro's takeover soon ends open cultural exchange between the two countries.
events.usContext: The Cuban Revolution succeeds. The U.S.-Cuba relationship rapidly and permanently deteriorates.

events.international: [concert] 1962-12-14 — Guantanamo Naval Base — CBS-TV, ”Ed Sullivan Show” (Stratemann p.483; Var December 26, 1962; Darensbourg p.214) Note: Stratemann

[concert] 1962-12-14 — Guantanamo Naval Base. Guantanamo Naval Base, Cuba, CBS-TV, "Ed Sullivan Show" Note: Stratemann and some other sources say December 23, 1962. Possibly the show was pretaped December 14 and telecast December 23, 1962. Context: While in Guantanamo Naval Base, The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) brings the world close to nuclear war.
events.usContext: In 1962, James Meredith integrates the University of Mississippi amid riots and federal intervention, spotlighting the battle over desegregation.

events.international: [concert] 1946-12-01 — Juarex. Juarex, Mexico Note: possibly a non-playing visit after the preceding nights performance?) Context: While in Juarex, Reconstruction and demobilization reshape societies after World War II.
events.usContext: In 1946, President Truman creates the President’s Committee on Civil Rights, signaling growing federal attention to civil rights.

events.international: Armstrong performs in Mexico City, drawing packed audiences of Mexican and American jazz fans. The Mexican press calls him "the king of music."
events.usContext: Truman desegregates the military. The Universal Declaration of Human Rights is signed at the UN.

events.international: Armstrong returns to Mexico City, now a jazz capital of Latin America, connecting with a new generation of Mexican musicians.
events.usContext: The Civil Rights movement intensifies with lunch counter sit-ins across the South.

events.international: [concert] 1954-12-23 — Barcelona — Windsor Palace. Barcelona, Spain, Windsor Palace, 3 concerts. (Stratemann p.615; booklet with CD Fresh Sound (Sp) FSRCD 3004) Note: tapes with recordings from these concerts were miraculously found and saved 40 years after the concerts. Context: While in Barcelona, The Cold War shapes politics and culture across continents in the mid-1950s.
events.usContext: In 1954, backlash to Brown begins to organize into “massive resistance” across the South.

events.international: [concert] 1955-12-23 — Barcelona — Windsor Palace, 3 concerts. (Stratemann p.615; booklet with CD Fresh Sound (Sp) FSRCD 3004) Note: tapes with recordings from these concerts were miraculously found
[context] Armstrong performs in Madrid under the Franco dictatorship—a complex cultural diplomacy moment that underscores jazz as a universal language.
events.usContext: The U.S. maintains a military base agreement with Francoist Spain as part of Cold War strategy, despite Spain's authoritarian government.
